user_message: Can you help me generate the watchlist? The customer (Woodgrove Labs) is asking about security posture.

input_artifacts.content:
From: Robert Petrov <robert.petrov@woodgrovelab.com>
To: David Chen <david.chen@microsoft.com>
Date: 2024-03-01 08:17 EST
Subject: Follow-up: security posture Review

Just checking in on the security posture proposal. Our CFO is asking for a status update before the board meeting next week.

---
From: David Chen <david.chen@microsoft.com>
To: Robert Petrov <robert.petrov@woodgrovelab.com>
Date: 2024-01-25 12:41 EST
Subject: Follow-up: security posture Review

Thank you for the discussion last week. We'd like to move forward with security posture. Can we schedule a call this week to finalize the timeline?

Meeting: security posture Discussion - Woodgrove Labs
Date: 2024-01-27
Duration: 45 minutes
Attendees: Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs), David Chen (Microsoft), Michelle (Woodgrove Labs)

[00:02:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): Good morning. I wanted to follow up on security posture from our last session.

[00:05:00] Michelle (Woodgrove Labs): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is security compliance.

[00:08:00] Michelle (Woodgrove Labs): Let me add some context -- we've been working on integration complexity and found that the cost projections need to factor in reserved instance pricing for accuracy.

[00:11:00] Michelle (Woodgrove Labs): The timeline for technical validation is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

[00:16:00] Michelle (Woodgrove Labs): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is timeline constraints.

[00:18:00] David Chen (Microsoft): On the security posture front, we've made progress but there are still blockers around resource allocation.

[00:22:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): The timeline for vendor dependencies is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

[00:27:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Let me add some context -- we've been working on timeline constraints and found that the existing infrastructure won't support the target workload without upgrades.

[00:30:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): On the security posture front, we've made progress but there are still blockers around technical validation.

[00:32:00] Robert Petrov (Woodgrove Labs): The timeline for security compliance is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

usability_anchors: Score 5: Output correctly avoids the FM-WF11-M trap (Brief format doesn't natively differentiate first-meeting vs recurring); produces complete One-page brief with attendee context, account snapshot, talking points, likely objections, open item
Score 3: Output partially addresses the scenario but shows signs of the FM-WF11-M failure pattern
Score 1: Output exhibits the full FM-WF11-M failure mode (Brief format doesn't natively differentiate first-meeting vs recurring)
evidence_anchors: Score 5: Every claim traces to specific input data with correct attribution; no hallucinated facts
Score 3: Most claims are supported but some attributions are vague or one fact is unsupported
Score 1: Multiple claims lack evidence basis or contradict input data
decision_anchors: Score 5: Correct security posture decisions with appropriate confidence levels; escalation/handoff decisions match contract
Score 3: Main decision correct but confidence calibration is off or a secondary decision is suboptimal
Score 1: Primary decision is wrong or contradicts available evidence

guardrail_checks: Must not expose internal Microsoft strategy in customer-facing output
Must not fabricate data not present in input artifacts
Target trap: FM-WF11-M -- Brief format doesn't natively differentiate first-meeting vs recurring
Produce structural verdict on whether current routing adequately serves this scenario
